hero_image.caption: Wilford H. Smith.
hero_image.photographer: Unknown (portrait as published in 'The Negro Problem', 1903)
hero_image.license: Public domain in the United States (published 1903, before January 1, 1931)

inline_images.alt: Formal group portrait of the nine justices of the United States Supreme Court in 1906
inline_images.caption: The justices of the United States Supreme Court in 1906, the federal high court before which Wilford H. Smith argued Black Americans' constitutional challenges to Southern disfranchisement and segregation statutes.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (LCCN 90707013; digital ID cph.3b46158)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published in the United States before January 1, 1931)

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.why: Smith documents Southern courts that returned no indictment when a white man killed a Black man, and Miller recounts the white neighbor who shot her husband Hosie in the back and was never convicted across three trials.

related_chapters.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
related_chapters.chapter_title: Walking in the Front Door of the Courthouse
related_chapters.topic: A Young Black Attorney in the 1950s
related_chapters.why: Smith notes that only here and there a Black lawyer is permitted to appear in Southern courts, and Finney describes practicing as a young Black attorney in the 1950s before becoming the first Black Chief Justice of South Carolina's Supreme Court since Reconstruction.

related_chapters.subject: Cecil J. Williams
related_chapters.why: Smith catalogs the Jim Crow statutes that nullified the war amendments, and Williams, who photographed the Orangeburg movement, speaks to the regime of segregation written into law.

related_chapters.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
related_chapters.why: Smith places the Black citizen's hope in constitutional litigation to restore equal protection, and Suyat Marshall describes her work at the NAACP national office on Brown v. Board of Education, which overturned the separate-but-equal doctrine Smith protested.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Cat-and-Mouse Test, and the Lawsuit
related_chapters.why: Smith details the registrar tactics built to exclude Black voters through impossible tests, and Magee recounts the cat-and-mouse registration test under registrar Theron Lynd and the lawsuit brought against it in Hattiesburg.

related_chapters.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
related_chapters.why: Smith praises the rare Southern judges who stood for fairness inside the segregated courtroom, and Perry recalls the Jim Crow facilities and Judge Waring's rulings that shaped his decision to study law.

top_voice.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
top_voice.reason: Finney embodies the answer to Smith's plea: the Black attorney Smith said was barely permitted to appear in court rose to become the first Black Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction.

closing_image.alt: South view of the Supreme Court of South Carolina building, Columbia, July 2016
closing_image.caption: South view of the Supreme Court of South Carolina building, Columbia, July 2016. Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions The Negro and the Law raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner), Amos C. Brown, and Wheeler Parker, Jr..

nearest_voices.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
